think:
Okay, so I'm Ava Johnson, a Meituan rider who's been working for a while now. Yesterday was another day on the job, and from what I understand, my performance was right around 11th out of 100 riders across several metrics: riding distance, money earned, and number of orders. That's decent but not stellar. 

Hmm, so let me think about how my working hours might have affected these stats. I started at 8 AM and finished at 6 PM yesterday. That’s a solid 10-hour shift. But if others are doing better than me in the same time frame, maybe there's something wrong with my approach.

Wait, could it be that the peak times aren't being utilized properly? Maybe during certain hours more orders come in, and by starting earlier or finishing later, I can capture those peaks. For instance, early mornings might have fewer riders online because people are just waking up, so I could get a head start on deliveries. Similarly, in the evening rush around dinner time, there's probably a surge in orders. If I adjust my schedule to cover these periods more effectively, maybe I can increase my order count and earnings.

Let me consider my current hours: 8 AM to 6 PM. That covers breakfast (maybe), lunch, and some of dinner time. But if others are starting earlier or staying later, they might be grabbing those extra orders during off-peak times when competition is lower but still have enough volume. 

So, maybe I should start an hour earlier at 7 AM to catch the early morning rush before most riders come online. And perhaps extend my shift by an hour until 7 PM to cover more of the dinner surge. That would give me a longer window and potentially access to more orders.

But wait, if everyone is starting earlier and staying later, will I just be competing in the same way? Or could there still be enough volume during those times that even with more riders, my efficiency can help me rank higher?

Also, considering distance ridden, perhaps by starting earlier, I can optimize my routes better, reducing unnecessary miles and making deliveries more efficiently. This might help improve my ranking not just in orders but also in earning potential.

Another thought: maybe working the same hours as most others is causing me to be stuck in a competitive period where it's harder to stand out. By adjusting my schedule slightly, I can avoid some of that competition during peak times and focus on less crowded periods when I can pick up more orders without as much rush.

So, putting this together, changing my working hours to start earlier at 7 AM might allow me to secure more orders before the main rush hits. Similarly, ending later at 7 PM could help me catch more deliveries during the dinner peak when demand is high. This adjustment should theoretically increase both the number of orders and the efficiency of my deliveries, which in turn could boost my earnings and riding performance.

I think I'll try starting an hour earlier today and finish an hour later. That way, I can test if this change makes a difference in my rankings compared to yesterday.

Alternatively, starting a bit earlier or working a slightly different shift could help her catch more orders. Maybe if she starts at 6:00 AM instead of 7:00 AM, she can cover the early morning rush and then continue through the lunch and dinner peaks. If she could focus on areas with higher order density, she might earn more without having to drive as far.

Maybe focusing on premium or higher-paying deliveries during peak times could increase her earnings. Alternatively, if she works slightly earlier shifts, she can capture more high-demand periods when each order might pay better.

So, balancing all these factors, maybe shifting her start time to 6:00 AM would allow her to get a head start on the morning rush and possibly position herself better for the lunch peak. This extended shift might help her increase both the number of orders and the earning potential, especially if those extra hours fall during peak times when more high-paying orders are available.

I should also think about whether there's any overlap in demand during her current shift that she's missing. Starting an hour earlier might not add much unless there's a significant morning rush, which can vary by location.

In conclusion, based on her current performance, slightly adjusting her working hours to start earlier and finish later during peak times seems like a reasonable strategy to increase her earnings without drastically changing her work duration.
